title: Memory Management & OOM Handling
description: Memory management is one of the most critical subsystems in Linux. The kernel manages physical RAM, virtual address spaces, page caches, swapping, and out-of-memory (OOM) handling. At Enterprise, memory-related issues (OOM kills, memory leaks, page cache pressure) are among the most common production incidents.

sections.title: Virtual Memory & Address Spaces
sections.description: Each process sees a virtual address space (typically 47 bits on x86_64 = 128TB). The kernel and hardware (MMU/TLB) translate virtual addresses to physical frames via page tables. This isolation is why one process can't read another's memory — and why mmap is so efficient (it maps files directly into the address space without copying).
sections.codeBlock:
# === Process memory map ===
cat /proc/<PID>/maps                 # Full memory map of a process
# Format: address-range  perms  offset  dev  inode  pathname

# Example interpretation:
# 555555554000-555555558000 r--p 00000000 fd:01 123456 /usr/bin/ls
# 555555558000-55555555c000 r-xp 00004000 fd:01 123456 /usr/bin/ls  (code)
# 7ffff7d80000-7ffff7f80000 rw-p 00000000 00:00 0                 (heap)
# 7ffffffde000-7ffffffff000 rw-p 00000000 00:00 0                 (stack)

# === Memory regions ===
# r-xp — Code segment (read, execute)
# r--p — Read-only data
# rw-p — Read-write data (heap, global vars)
# rw-p [stack] — Thread stack
# rw-p [heap]  — Heap (brk/mmap allocations)

# === /proc/<PID>/status memory fields ===
cat /proc/<PID>/status | grep -i vm
# VmPeak: peak virtual memory size
# VmSize: current virtual memory size
# VmLck:  locked (mlock) memory
# VmPin:  pinned memory
# VmHWM:  peak resident set size
# VmRSS:  current resident set size (= actual physical RAM used)
# RssAnon: anonymous memory (heap, stack, mmap without file)
# RssFile: file-backed memory (mmap of files, shared libraries)
# RssShmem: shared memory
# VmSwap: swapped memory

# === pmap — detailed process memory ===
pmap -x <PID>                        # Extended memory map with RSS/DClean
pmap -XX <PID>                       # Very detailed (kernel flags)

# === Total memory usage ===
free -h                              # Total, used, free, shared, buff/cache, available
cat /proc/meminfo                    # Detailed breakdown
# MemTotal: total physical RAM
# MemFree: completely free
# Buffers: block device cache
# Cached: page cache + tmpfs + shmem
# SwapTotal: total swap
# SwapFree: free swap
# Available: estimated memory available for starting new apps
#             (= MemFree + reclaimable page cache + reclaimable slab)

# === Slab allocator (kernel memory) ===
cat /proc/slabinfo                   # Kernel slab allocations
# Slab is the kernel's memory allocator for small objects (inodes, dentries, etc.)
# High slab usage can indicate kernel memory leak
sudo slabtop -s c                    # Sort by cache size

sections.title: Page Cache & Buffers
sections.description: Linux uses all available free RAM for page cache (file data cached from disk) and buffers (block device metadata). This is not 'wasted' memory — it improves performance dramatically. When an application needs memory, the kernel reclaims page cache pages. At Enterprise, a system with '99% memory used' is normal and healthy — what matters is the 'available' metric.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Page cache basics ===
# When you read a file, the kernel caches it in page cache
# Subsequent reads are served from RAM (fast) instead of disk (slow)

# === Drop caches (for benchmarking only!) ===
# NEVER do this in production — it destroys performance temporarily
sudo sync && echo 3 | sudo tee /proc/sys/vm/drop_caches
# 1 = free page cache, 2 = free dentries/inodes, 3 = free both

# === Page cache statistics ===
cat /proc/vmstat | grep -E "(pgfault|pgmajfault|pgfree)"
# pgfault   — Minor page faults (page was in memory, just needed mapping)
# pgmajfault — Major page faults (page had to be read from disk)
# High major faults = slow I/O, insufficient memory

# === File-backed memory vs anonymous ===
# file-backed: mmap'd files, executable code, shared libraries
# anonymous: heap, stack, anonymous mmap (mallloc uses this)
# Key difference: file-backed can be reclaimed (write dirty pages to disk);
#   anonymous must be swapped to disk (slow!) or the process must be killed

# === vmtouch — page cache analysis ===
sudo apt install vmtouch
vmtouch /var/log/syslog              # How much of a file is in cache
vmtouch /usr/bin/*                   # Page cache stats for directory
# Files that are frequently accessed stay in cache (good)
# Cache can be 'poisoned' by a one-time large read (bad)

# === fincore — which pages are cached ===
# (part of linux-tools-common)
# fincore --pages=false --summarize /var/log/syslog

# === Tune page cache behavior ===
# /etc/sysctl.conf:
# vm.vfs_cache_pressure=100          # Default: reclaim slab at same rate as page cache
#   Lower = keep more dentry/inode cache (good for many small files)
#   Higher = reclaim slab more aggressively
# vm.min_free_kbytes=65536            # Reserve memory for critical allocations
# vm.dirty_ratio=20                   # Max dirty pages % before blocking writers
# vm.dirty_background_ratio=10        # Background writeback starts at this % dirty
# vm.swappiness=10                    # Only swap when absolutely necessary (default: 60)

sections.title: OOM Killer: Out-Of-Memory Management
sections.description: When the system runs out of memory, the kernel's OOM killer selects a process to kill to free memory. Understanding OOM behavior is critical — it's often the root cause of mysterious service failures. At Enterprise, OOM kills are monitored closely, and services are configured with cgroup memory limits to prevent a single service from starving the system.
sections.codeBlock:
# === OOM logs ===
dmesg | grep -i "oom_killer\|OOM"    # Find OOM events
# Output includes:
# - Total memory, free memory
# - Which process was killed (PID, name, score)
# - Memory footprint of top processes

# === OOM score ===
cat /proc/<PID>/oom_score            # OOM score (higher = more likely to be killed)
# Score is based on: RSS + swap + page table + (total memory * factor)
cat /proc/<PID>/oom_score_adj        # Adjust score (-1000 to 1000)
# Services you want to protect: echo -1000 > /proc/<PID>/oom_score_adj
# Services you want killed first: echo 1000 > /proc/<PID>/oom_score_adj

# === OOM in systemd units ===
[Service]
# Protect critical services:
OOMScoreAdjust=-500

# === OOM in cgroups ===
# cgroups v2 memory.max will trigger OOM kill within the cgroup,
# without affecting other cgroups. This is the PREFERRED approach at Enterprise.
echo "100M" > /sys/fs/cgroup/myapp/memory.max

# === How the OOM killer selects a victim ===
# 1. Calculates oom_score for each process
#    - Based on RSS, swap, page table size
#    - Root processes get a 3% bonus (less likely to be killed)
# 2. Selects the process with the highest score
# 3. Sends SIGKILL
# 4. If memory still insufficient, repeats

# === Prevent OOM: reserve swap ===
# Swap is slower than RAM but provides a safety buffer
# Without swap, OOM killer activates sooner
# At Enterprise, swap is often disabled — memory limits are enforced via cgroups
# But understanding swap is still essential:
swapon --show                        # Check swap status
sudo swapon /swapfile                # Enable swap file

# === Memory overcommit ===
cat /proc/sys/vm/overcommit_memory
# 0 = heuristic overcommit (default — conservative)
# 1 = always overcommit (dangerous!)
# 2 = no overcommit (vm.overcommit_ratio controls %)
# At Enterprise: usually 0 or 2, never 1

# === Check for OOM in systemd journal ===
journalctl -k | grep -i "oom\|killed"
journalctl -u myapp | grep -i "killed\|memory"

sections.title: Huge Pages & Memory Performance
sections.description: The default page size is 4KB. Huge pages (2MB or 1GB) reduce TLB misses and improve performance for memory-intensive applications (databases, JVMs, packet processing). At Enterprise, databases and JVM-based services commonly use huge pages. There are two types: Transparent Huge Pages (THP) and explicit Huge Pages (hugetlbfs).
sections.codeBlock:
# === Huge page sizes ===
cat /proc/meminfo | grep Huge
# HugePages_Total: 256           # 256 × 2MB = 512MB allocated
# HugePages_Free: 128
# Hugepagesize: 2048 kB

# === Configure explicit huge pages ===
echo 256 | sudo tee /proc/sys/vm/nr_hugepages
# Persistent: vm.nr_hugepages=256 in /etc/sysctl.conf

# === Mount hugetlbfs ===
sudo mkdir /mnt/huge
sudo mount -t hugetlbfs hugetlbfs /mnt/huge
# Now applications can mmap files in /mnt/huge with 2MB pages

# === Transparent Huge Pages (THP) ===
cat /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled
# [always] madvise never
# always — kernel tries to use 2MB pages automatically
# madvise — applications request huge pages via madvise()
# never — disable THP

# === THP and databases ===
# THP can cause latency spikes (background defragmentation)
# Many databases (MongoDB, PostgreSQL) recommend disabling THP

# To disable THP:
sudo sh -c 'echo never > /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled'
sudo sh -c 'echo never > /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/defrag'

# === Per-process hugepages ===
# JVM: -XX:+UseLargePages -XX:LargePageSizeInBytes=2m
# PostgreSQL: huge_pages = on
# MongoDB: disable THP (uses its own WiredTiger cache management)

# === Memory access patterns ===
# Sequential access: page cache prefetches ahead (readahead)
# Random access: high TLB miss rate, benefits from huge pages
# NUMA-local access: always prefer allocating memory on the same socket
numactl --membind 0 --cpunodebind 0 ./myapp  # Bind memory + CPU to node 0

sections.title: Interview Q&A
sections.description:
Q: 'Free shows 99% memory used but the system is running fine. Is this a problem?'

A: 'Not necessarily. Linux uses free memory for page cache and buffers — this is not 'used' memory in the traditional sense. The key metric is 'available' (in /proc/meminfo or free -h), which estimates memory reclaimable without swapping. If 'available' is low and swap usage is growing, then you have a problem. At Enterprise, we monitor 'available memory' rather than 'free memory' and set alerts when it drops below a threshold (e.g., 10% of total).'

Q: 'A process was killed by OOM. How do you investigate?'

A: 'Check dmesg | grep -i oom to find the OOM event — it shows the victim (PID, name), total memory, and the top memory consumers at the time. Check if the process had cgroup memory limits that were too low. Check if there was a memory leak using ps aux --sort=-%mem | head before and after restart. Check /var/log/ for any evidence of the leak. If it's a Java process, analyze the heap dump. Root cause is almost always: (1) memory leak in application code, (2) cgroup limit set too low, or (3) traffic spike causing legitimate increased memory usage.'

Q: 'What is the difference between buffers and cache in /proc/meminfo?'

A: 'Buffers are metadata associated with block devices (e.g., filesystem metadata like superblock, inode tables). Cache is the page cache — actual file data cached from disk. The practical difference: 'Buffers' is relatively small and stable, while 'Cached' can be gigabytes and fluctuates based on file access patterns. Both are reclaimable, which is why 'available' combines them with free memory. In modern kernels (5.x+), the distinction is less meaningful than it used to be, but the fields are kept for compatibility.'

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Kernel VM Documentation — All vm sysctl parameters explained
  • Understanding the Linux Kernel — O'Reilly, chapters on memory management
  • Linux Memory Management (Mel Gorman) — Classic deep-dive into the VM subsystem

analogy.title: Memory as a Library with a Cache
analogy.description: RAM is the reading room - fast but limited. Swap is the basement archive - slow but vast. Page cache keeps recently read books on the desk. OOM killer is the librarian who ejects readers when the room is full. Huge pages are oversized reference books that stay open on the desk.

commonMistakes.mistake: Ignoring log rotation until disk fills up
commonMistakes.solution: Configure logrotate before deploying; monitor /var/log usage

commonMistakes.mistake: Disabling SELinux/AppArmor instead of fixing policies
commonMistakes.solution: Use `audit2allow` to generate policies from denials; keep MAC enabled
Add `USER nonroot` in Dockerfile; use `--user` flag at runtime

commonMistakes.mistake: Not setting resource limits on containers
commonMistakes.solution: Always set `--cpus` and `--memory`; configure cgroup limits in Kubernetes

practiceLabs.title: Explore: Virtual Memory & Address Spaces
practiceLabs.task: Run `cat` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man cat` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

practiceLabs.title: Explore: Page Cache & Buffers
practiceLabs.task: Run `pmap` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man pmap` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

practiceLabs.title: Explore: OOM Killer: Out-Of-Memory Management
practiceLabs.task: Run `free` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man free` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

quickReference.title: Quick Reference
quickReference.content:
| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `cat` | Virtual Memory & Address Spaces |
| `pmap` | Virtual Memory & Address Spaces |
| `free` | Virtual Memory & Address Spaces |
| `sudo` | Page Cache & Buffers |
| `cat` | Page Cache & Buffers |
| `vmtouch` | Page Cache & Buffers |
| `dmesg` | OOM Killer: Out-Of-Memory Management |
| `cat` | OOM Killer: Out-Of-Memory Management |
| `OOMScoreAdjust` | OOM Killer: Out-Of-Memory Management |
| `cat` | Huge Pages & Memory Performance |
| `echo` | Huge Pages & Memory Performance |
| `sudo` | Huge Pages & Memory Performance |

troubleshooting.cause: Incorrect arguments, wrong working directory, or stale cached data
troubleshooting.solution: Verify arguments with `man cat`; check current path with `pwd`; clear cache if applicable

troubleshooting.cause: Service not reloaded or syntax error in config file
troubleshooting.solution: Validate config syntax with built-in test flag; reload service with `systemctl reload` or `restart`

troubleshooting.symptom: memory operation slow or timing out
troubleshooting.cause: Resource exhaustion (disk I/O, memory pressure, or lock contention)
troubleshooting.solution: Check `iostat`, `free -m`, and `top`; look for stuck processes with `ps aux --sort=-%cpu`

troubleshooting.cause: Incorrect file ownership or overly restrictive permissions
troubleshooting.solution: Inspect with `ls -la`; fix with `chown`/ `chmod`; check ACLs with `getfacl`

quiz.question: What is Virtual Memory & Address Spaces?
options: A standardized directory structure that defines where files and directories should be placed on Linux systems
A tool for managing disk partitions
quiz.answer: A standardized directory structure that defines where files and directories should be placed on Linux systems
quiz.explanation: The Virtual Memory & Address Spaces is the foundation of the Linux filesystem — everything in the system is organized under this structure.

quiz.question: What does `cat` do?
quiz.answer: Lists files and directories in the current or specified path
quiz.explanation: Use `ls` to explore directory contents. Combine with flags like `-l` (long format), `-a` (hidden files), and `-h` (human-readable sizes).

quiz.question: Which command would you use to view disk usage of the memory directory?
quiz.explanation: `du -sh` (disk usage, summary, human-readable) shows the total size of a directory and its contents.

quiz.question: A user reports "Permission denied" when trying to access a memory directory. What is the first command you should run?
quiz.explanation: `ls -ld` shows the directory's permissions and ownership. Always inspect before making changes — starting with `chmod 777` is a security anti-pattern.

quiz.question: In a memory scenario, your monitoring alerts show cat failing intermittently. What is the correct debugging workflow?
options: Check logs → verify configuration → test in staging → apply fix → monitor
Ignore the alert and wait for it to resolve
quiz.answer: Check logs → verify configuration → test in staging → apply fix → monitor
quiz.explanation: A structured debugging workflow prevents cascading failures. Always diagnose before acting, especially in production environments where downtime has direct business impact.

interviewTips: FAANG Architect's Note:  Memory management is the subsystem I've seen cause the most baffling production issues
FAANG,  every  service runs in a cgroup with a memory limit that's 80-90% of its expected max
productionTips: Production Tip:  If you're using JVM, Go, or any garbage-collected runtime, always set both heap and cgroup memory limits
bestPractices: Use version control for all configuration (/etc, scripts, Dockerfiles)
Implement infrastructure as code; avoid manual server changes
Test changes in staging before production; have rollback plans
enterprisePerspective: In enterprise environments, memory management & oom handling is managed through automation (Ansible, Terraform), monitored centrally (Prometheus, Datadog), and governed by compliance policies (CIS benchmarks, SOC2). Changes go through change advisory boards with rollback procedures documented.
